conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex who is 14, and Blake who is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask a few more questions.

Let's start with Blake, who is 19. Is Blake currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in approved education. He's doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information. 

For Blake doing A-levels at sixth form, I need to check: did Blake start (or was accepted/enrolled for) this approved education or training before turning 19 years old?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he did. He started the course before he turned 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to ask about your living situation with the children.

Do both Alex and Blake live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't both live with me. Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't - Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Since Alex lives with their other parent, I need to ask: are you contributing towards Alex's upkeep at all? For example, are you paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay (currently at least £25.60 per week for one child or £16.95 per week if you have another child you're claiming for)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not contributing financially towards Alex at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me analyze your situation for each child:

**For Blake (19 years old):** Blake is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), started the course before turning 19, and lives with you. You can claim Child Benefit for a young person aged 16-19 if they're in approved education or training that started before they turned 20 (or they were accepted or enrolled before turning 20). Blake meets these criteria.

**For Alex (14 years old):** Although Alex is under 16, they live with their other parent, and you're not contributing financially towards their upkeep. Child Benefit is generally paid to the person the child lives with. Since Alex doesn't live with you and you're not contributing at least the amount equivalent to Child Benefit, you would not be eligible to claim for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **Summary:** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake, but not for Alex. You should be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake as long as he continues in approved education or training, which can continue until the Monday after his 20th birthday (as long as the course hasn't finished).

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 14 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
circumstances: Someone who lives with the child is already claiming
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 14 years old (under 16) but lives with their other parent, not with the claimant. The claimant is not contributing financially towards Alex's upkeep. Under Child Benefit rules, the benefit is normally paid to the person the child lives with. Since Alex does not live with the claimant and the claimant does not contribute at least the equivalent amount of Child Benefit towards Alex's upkeep, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 19 years old and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college). Blake started this course before turning 19 years old and lives with the claimant. Under Child Benefit rules, you can claim for a young person aged 16-19 if they are in approved education or training that started (or they were accepted/enrolled) before they turned 20. Blake meets all these criteria, therefore the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (14) and Blake (19). The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake only. Blake qualifies as he is 19, in approved education (A-levels) that started before he turned 19, and lives with the claimant. The claimant is not eligible for Alex because Alex lives with their other parent and the claimant does not contribute financially towards Alex's upkeep. Child Benefit is normally paid to the person the child lives with.
